title: The Eye of the Needle
subtitle: Christ on the hard road of the rich, and the reward of those who leave all

stillness: Be still, and let the noise of the world fall away.
Chrysostom warns: do not lie back idle, as if to escape what is impossible —
but, weighing the greatness of God's righteousness, leap up, and pray.
gospel: that a rich man shall hardly enter into the kingdom of heaven."
It is easier for a camel to pass through the eye of a needle,
than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of heaven."
Hearing this, the disciples wondered exceedingly, saying:
And Jesus, beholding them, said to them:
but with God all things are possible."
behold: Jesus had seen the young man go away sad,
drawn back by the persuasion of his riches.
Taking occasion from this man who had possessions,
He begins to speak of the covetous man under the name of the rich.
He would steady His disciples in the perfection they had taken up,
that it might not seem hard to them
that, leaving all things, they had followed Christ.
The disciples, themselves poor and without means,
hearing these words, and pitying their fellow men,
in the zeal of charity longing for their salvation,
wondered, and asked: Who then can be saved?
And Jesus, looking on them with an eye gentle and meek,
tempered the severity of the sentence with His own mercy,
soothed their fearful minds, and consoled them.
consider: He does not say it is impossible, but hard:
for it is hard to possess riches and not be held too fast by love of them.
As Augustine says: earthly goods are loved more tightly once gained than while desired;
it is one thing not to take in what is lacking — another to tear out what is already grafted in;
the first are cast off as strangers, the second are cut away as one's own limbs.
And Chrysostom: the heaping up of riches heaps up the greater flame, and the longing grows more violent.
These are the thorns and briers that choke the seed of God.
Therefore it is safe neither to have nor to love riches.
Naked we come to the font, that naked we may hasten to the gate of heaven;
how unfitting, how absurd, that one whom his mother bore naked,
whom the Church received naked, should wish to enter heaven rich!
When a man clings to riches with love, possessing them apart from God,
so that he is hindered from the free following of Christ — hardly does he enter.
But when he trusts in riches, sets his faith and hope in them,
grows proud and wanton, holding them greedily, with love above God,
stretching out no hand to the needy — for such a one it is impossible.
The whole camel God does not bring through; divided, broken into small parts, He passes it.
So the rich man cannot enter by the narrow way,
unless his riches be laid down — cast away, or at least no longer loved.
Let us flee, then, this worst greed, which pins us to the earth,
and will not let us enter the kingdom of heaven and be saved.
With men, who of themselves can fall but not of themselves rise, this is impossible;
but with God all things are possible —
He can part the heart from riches by His grace,
and turn a man from the love of earthly things to the love of heavenly.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, grant to me, wretched and unworthy,
that for Your sake and for love of You,
and for the glorifying of Your name,
I may be able to leave riches, and delights, and pomps,
and all things that are of the world,
and, among all things, my very self;
and, all else shut out, to cleave to You alone;
and, according to my small measure, to follow You
by whatever imitation of living I can,
that — with You as ruler, with You as guide —
I may deserve to escape the snares and ambushes
of all my enemies, visible and invisible,
which You have promised to those who leave all things and follow You. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Nam ideo nudi accedimus ad lavacrum, ut nudi ad cœli januam properemus.
latinJewel.english: For we come naked to the font, that naked we may hasten to the gate of heaven.
